skills.name: TypeScript Strict Mode Configuration
skills.description: Configure TypeScript with strict mode flags for maximum type safety. Strict mode catches entire classes of bugs at compile time.
skills.template:
## TypeScript Strict Mode

```json
// tsconfig.json — recommended strict config
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "strict": true,                        // enables all strict* flags
    "noUncheckedIndexedAccess": true,      // arr[0] is T | undefined, not T
    "exactOptionalPropertyTypes": true,    // { a?: string } !== { a: string | undefined }
    "noImplicitReturns": true,
    "noFallthroughCasesInSwitch": true,
    "noPropertyAccessFromIndexSignature": true,
    "allowUnusedLabels": false,
    "allowUnreachableCode": false
  }
}
```

`strict: true` enables:
- `strictNullChecks` — `null`/`undefined` not assignable to non-nullable types
- `strictFunctionTypes` — function parameter contravariance
- `strictBindCallApply` — typed `bind`/`call`/`apply`
- `strictPropertyInitialization` — class properties must be initialized
- `noImplicitAny` — prohibits implicit `any`
- `noImplicitThis` — `this` must be typed

**Incremental adoption:** add `// @ts-strict-ignore` per file when migrating a legacy codebase.

skills.name: unknown vs any — Safe Error and External Data Handling
skills.description: Use unknown instead of any for external data, error catches, and API responses. Narrows safely with type guards before use.

skills.name: Discriminated Unions for State Machines
skills.description: Model mutually exclusive states with discriminated unions. TypeScript narrows exhaustively, eliminating impossible-state bugs.

skills.name: Generic Constraints and Conditional Types
skills.description: Write reusable, type-safe generics with extends constraints and conditional types. Avoid unsafe overloads.

skills.name: Built-in Utility Types
skills.description: Use TypeScript's built-in utility types to avoid hand-writing redundant type transformations.

skills.name: satisfies Operator — Type-checked Literals
skills.description: Use satisfies to validate a value against a type while keeping the literal type. Avoids widening that loses specific values.

skills.name: Template Literal Types
skills.description: Use template literal types to type event names, CSS class names, API paths, and other string patterns at the type level.

skills.name: Readonly and as const for Immutability
skills.description: Use Readonly<T>, as const, and ReadonlyArray to enforce immutability in TypeScript, preventing accidental mutation.
